title: Middle East crisis: US military says service member killed during ‘controlled detonation’ of downed Iranian drone – as it happened
summary: This live blog is now closed.
body: The US service member killed during the disposal of an Iranian drone in Iraq on Saturday was not a bomb technician but another soldier who was hit by debris or fragments when the drone was detonated, a US official has told the New York Times. The incident is under investigation, according to the official. US Central Command said on Sunday that a US service member in northern Iraq was killed in action on Saturday during a controlled detonation of a downed Iranian drone. It comes after two US soldiers were killed and one missing by an Iranian attack on a military facility in Jordan. Centcom said in an update shared on X: A U.S. service member in northern Iraq was killed in action July 18 during a controlled detonation of unexploded ordnance from a downed Iranian one-way attack drone. A second service member was wounded and continues to receive medical treatment for a minor injury. It added that it had located unidentified remains in Jordan after a service member was listed as missing in action. “An examination process to verify the remains is ongoing,” Centcom said. It brings the total number of US service members who have died since the US and Israel launched war against Iran five months ago to 17. More than 430 US troops have been wounded. The US military launched further strikes after vowing to “swiftly punish” Iran for an attack on Jordan that left two US service members dead and one missing, as fears deepened on Sunday that the war could spiral further into a wider regional conflict. Iran said the United States had attacked an under-construction nuclear power plant in Khuzestan province, in the country’s southwest. The UN’s nuclear watchdog, the IAEA urged restraint and said it was looking into the reports. The US’s allies in the region also reported a fresh wave of Iranian fire. In Kuwait, officials said a power and water distillation plant was attacked for a second consecutive day. Air defences also intercepted strikes in Jordan and Bahrain. Jordan’s foreign ministry summoned the chargé d’affaires of the Iranian embassy in Amman on Sunday to deliver “a strongly worded protest message” about the attacks. Meanwhile, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, met with Lebanese president Joseph Aoun in Washington on Sunday to discuss the implementation of the US-backed trilateral framework. Rubio hailed the country for its “move towards peace” after the latest round of Lebanon-Israel talks. Aoun is due to also meet with Donald Trump on Tuesday in Washington. And the United States was sending more warplanes to the Middle East, the New York Times (paywall) reported, citing US officials who spoke on the condition of anonymity on Sunday, another sign the Trump administration may soon further escalate its strikes against Iran. The redeployment of the aircraft had already been ordered before the Iranian attack on a military facility in Jordan that killed two US service members. Iraqi prime minister Ali al-Zaidi is scheduled to visit Iran later this week following a trip to the US where he met with Donald Trump, AFP reports, citing Iraqi state media, on Sunday. Washington and Tehran are Iraq’s main allies, but their enmity has long turned the country into a proxy battleground and left successive governments struggling to maintain a delicate balance between the foes, who have traded intensifying attacks in recent days. Iraq’s national news agency, INA, quoted the foreign ministry as saying al-Zaidi will visit Tehran “at the end of this week” and is expected to sign memorandums of understanding “to strengthen bilateral ties”. It will be his first trip to Iran since coming to power this year with Washington’s blessing after Trump vetoed another candidate. Al-Zaidi returned to Iraq on Sunday after his one-week trip to the United States, having struck 48 agreements and partnerships with American companies. A businessman, he has vowed to boost Iraq’s fragile economy and disarm pro-Iran groups that have targeted US facilities. In recent days, attacks blamed on Iran have rocked Iraq’s autonomous Kurdistan region, which hosts US troops and foreign oil companies, as well as exiled Iranian Kurdish rebels. The United States was sending more warplanes to the Middle East, the New York Times (paywall) reports, citing US officials who spoke on the condition of anonymity on Sunday, another sign the Trump administration may soon further escalate its strikes against Iran. According to the officials, Air Force F-16 fighter jets from Germany and stealthy F-35 jets from the UK were being sent to the region, along with additional aerial refuelling aircraft. US Central Command, which oversees operations in the region, declined to comment to the NYT on the reported deployments. The redeployment of the aircraft had already been ordered before an Iranian attack on Friday that killed two US Army soldiers, left another service member missing, and injured many people in Jordan. That attack came as the two sides traded strikes and counter-strikes all week long, and prompted retaliation by the United States that the military said was in part to “swiftly punish Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps forces”. Even before those strikes, Donald Trump signalled that he planned to increase attacks on Iran in the coming week, and intended to hit more Iranian infrastructure, including bridges and power plants. A reminder that targeting civilian infrastructure without a clear military target could constitute a war crime. It was not immediately clear where all the additional warplanes would be stationed, but an Israeli military official who spoke on the condition of anonymity on Saturday told the New York Times the United States had decided to place some additional aerial refuelling aircraft at Israeli Air Force bases. Jordan’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs summoned the chargé d’affaires of the Iranian embassy in Amman on Sunday to deliver “a strongly worded protest message” about Iranian attacks. The ministry also took issue with “provocative and inflammatory statements” made by official Iranian entities, it said in a statement. Foreign ministry spokesperson Fuad Al-Majali said the ministry instructed the Iranian diplomat to relay “a clear message” to Tehran to immediately halt its attacks on the kingdom, the statement added. “Jordan’s security and the safety of its citizens represent a red line that cannot be crossed,” he said, according to the statement. Earlier on Sunday, Jordanian air defenses intercepted three Iranian missiles on Sunday afternoon local time while a fourth landed in a remote area, the country’s military said. Further to that last post, Lebanese officials said president Joseph Aoun is due to meet with Donald Trump on Tuesday in Washington. While in Washington, Aoun planned talks “on the situation in Lebanon and ways to strengthen the ceasefire” as well as on “the withdrawal of Israel from the Lebanese regions it occupies,” his office said earlier. As we reported earlier, US secretary of state Marco Rubio met with Lebanese president Joseph Aoun in Washington on Sunday to discuss the implementation of the US-backed trilateral framework, hailing the country for its “move towards peace” after the latest round of Lebanon-Israel talks. Rubio “commended the courage of the Government of Lebanon, under President Aoun’s leadership, for their determined effort to reclaim Lebanon’s sovereignty, disarm Hezbollah and dismantle its terrorist infrastructure, and move towards peace”, the US state department said in a statement. It added that Rubio reaffirmed the US’s commitment to supporting the implementation of the framework. The UN’s nuclear watchdog called for restraint on Sunday after Iran’s Atomic Energy Organization said the United States had attacked an under-construction nuclear power plant in the country’s south-west. The US and Iran have been trading escalating attacks for days, and Washington carried out fresh airstrikes on Sunday to “punish” Iran for the deaths of two US service members in Jordan on Friday – its first reported losses since the return to open hostilities in the war. Tehran’s Atomic Energy Organization said US forces “in an aggressive and brutal act contrary to international law, attacked the under-construction Darkhovin nuclear power plant... with a number of projectiles on Sunday”, according to a statement carried by state TV. As we reported earlier, the UN’s International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) said it was looking into the reports, noting the plant “is in the very early stages of construction and contained no nuclear material when last visited by the IAEA”. While the incident “is not believed to pose any radiological risk”, IAEA director Rafael Grossi reiterated his “call for military restraint in the vicinity of all nuclear-related sites”, the agency said, in a post on X. The US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, met with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun in Washington on Sunday, a state department official told the AFP news agency. It is the first trip to Washington by a Lebanese head of state since Michel Sleiman was received by former US president Barack Obama in 2009. The Lebanese president’s office said Aoun would meet Donald Trump on Tuesday. Aoun, who served as the commander of Lebanon’s US-backed army before ‌being elected president last year, will reportedly present a plan to Trump on how to disarm Hezbollah and secure Israel’s withdrawal from southern Lebanon. Hezbollah has firmly rejected the government’s direct talks with Israel and efforts by the state, which it operates independently of, to strip it of its arms. As well is killing many Lebanese civilians in airstrikes across the country, the Israeli military has destroyed dozens of villages in the areas it occupies, something Human Rights Watch said could amount to a war crime. Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese people remain displaced from their homes. Israel has occupied large swathes of south Lebanon in what it calls a “security zone” and says it is trying to prote…

title: Dozens missing after passenger ferry sinks off coast of Guyana
summary: Emergency response has rescued 67 people, including 15 children, from one of the country’s worst maritime disasters
body: A ferry carrying 116 passengers and crew sank ⁠off the coast of Guyana in South America ⁠late Saturday, with 67 ⁠people, including 15 children, rescued so far, according to Guyana ⁠authorities. A massive search-and-rescue operation continues for dozens of missing passengers in what has been described by officials as one of the country’s worst maritime disasters. The ferry MV Barima left Georgetown, the capital of the English-speaking South American country, on Saturday at about 3.15pm for Port Kaituma, located in the oil-rich Essequibo region and just off the border with Venezuela. A distress call was received shortly after 11pm, prompting an emergency response involving the Guyana Defence Force Coast Guard and Air Corps, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers. Juan Edghill, the minister of public works, said preliminary investigations indicate the vessel was operating within its legal limits. “There was no engine problem or any mechanical problem. It had nothing to do with overload. It had to do with the tide,” Edghill told local reporters. He said the MV Barima was certified to carry 397 passengers and 284 tonnes of cargo. Its manifest showed it was transporting 268 tonnes of cargo, below its authorised capacity. The ship was equipped with 250 lifejackets, two lifeboats and six inflatable rafts, Edghill said. In a statement on Sunday, Guyana’s prime minister, Mark Phillips, said the search and rescue coordination centre had expanded the search area from 400 sq km (154 sq miles) to 1,070 sq km, with additional aircraft and vessels deployed alongside local fisherfolk familiar with the waters. The 67 survivors include 41 men, 11 women and 15 children. As the rescue effort continues, survivors recounted the moments when the ferry overturned in darkness. Adam Matabheek, who was travelling with his wife, brother, sister-in-law and three young nieces and nephews, said he noticed the vessel leaning several hours before it capsized. “We dismissed it as rough water and went to sleep,” he said. Moments later, the vessel rolled over. “I ended up in the water,” he recalled, saying he managed to grab a lifejacket before drifting through the night. His wife, Carlita Matabheek, who cannot swim, said she became trapped inside the overturned vessel before a lifejacket fell within her reach. “I started to pray,” she said. After calling repeatedly for her husband in the darkness, she felt someone tugging at her hair. “It was Adam,” she said. The couple and Adam’s younger brother, Nathaniel, spent the night drifting in the sea until rescuers found them after daybreak. “God cleared the skies and saved us,” Carlita said. Their relief, however, was overshadowed by tragedy. Nathaniel’s wife, Rachel Welch, and their three children – Nathaniel Jr., 8, Natalia, 5, and Ezekiel, 3 – remain among the missing. According to the family, Rachel was thrown from her hammock as the ferry capsized and tried desperately to reach her children before they disappeared into the water. Another survivor, Loureen Singh, a trader who regularly travels to Port Kaituma to sell clothing, survived by holding on to the same lifejacket as Carlita after telling her she could swim “a little”. Carlita urged her: “Hold on to me.” The two women remained together throughout the night until they were rescued. Authorities say families of all recorded passengers and crew members have been contacted as efforts continue to account for everyone aboard.

title: Mission in Iran war has pivoted toward ensuring oil flow, Trump official says
summary: Energy secretary grapples with mounting criticism over length of US war as Democrat calls conflict ‘war of choice’
body: A Trump administration official on Sunday grappled with mounting criticism over the length of the US war with Iran after months of promised diplomatic breakthroughs, short-lived ceasefires, and the deaths of three more American troops bringing the total number of American military members killed in the conflict to 17. Appearing on ABC’s This Week, the US energy secretary, Chris Wright, defended the Trump administration’s strategy after being confronted with his earlier claims that the war which began in February would have ended by now. Wright argued that the mission has since shifted toward ensuring oil shipments can move through the strait of Hormuz. “We did a pause to try to get an agreement with the Iranian people to allow oil to flow without them attacking it,” Wright said. “They’ve proven unwilling to do that, and we’ve pivoted. Now, what we’re doing is assuring that that oil and gas and other products can flow through the strait of Hormuz with or without Iranian cooperation, and we’ve continued to degrade their offensive military capabilities.” On the same program, a prominent Democrat continued his party’s sharp criticism of the Trump administration’s handling of the nearly five-month conflict. US senator Mark Warner of Virginia described the conflict as a “war of choice” that was launched without an imminent threat and argued the administration has failed to accomplish its previously stated objectives. “Regime change, how are we doing there? We got a worse crowd than before,” Warner said of Iran. He said the enriched uranium that the US wanted to get from Iran to prevent it from building a nuclear weapon is “still there”, adding: “Matter of fact, it would take a minimum of 15,000 troops on the ground for a week to try to take these canisters, which are quite volatile, out of their bunker.” Warner also remarked, “Getting rid of Iran’s missile and drone capability – obviously, we’ve not been successful there” because the conflict continued on Sunday. He said: “The continuation of this war, at this pace, will be a continuing disaster.” In addition to two deaths in Jordan on Friday, the US Central Command announced that another service member ‌was killed on Saturday “during a controlled detonation of ⁠unexploded ordnance” in northern Iraq. The unexploded ordnance ‌was ⁠from a “downed Iranian one-way attack drone”, Centcom said in a ⁠statement on social media on Sunday. The latest casualties in Iran for the US military came as a renewal of the campaign against Iran entered its second week, leading to intensified fighting around the strait of Hormuz. The US launched retaliatory strikes on Sunday against Iran’s Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) after the killings. After the deaths of the two US troops on Friday, Trump’s defense secretary Pete Hegseth vowed the military campaign in Iran would continue, writing on social media that the service members’ “sacrifice only stiffens our resolve”. Donald Trump himself also escalated pressure on Iran Sunday, with the president urging Congress to include Iran in pending Russia sanctions legislation. The move would further weaken the memorandum of understanding between the two countries in June aimed at resolving the Iranian conflict that included an agreement to end sanctions for Iran. Alluding to the unexpected death on 11 July of US senator Lindsey Graham, his ally, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform, “Republicans should add Iran to the Russian Sanctions Bill. That’s what Lindsey wanted to do, and it was going to happen. IMPORTANT!!!”

title: Burnham to say UK must be honest about its challenges as he prepares to become PM
summary: Labour leader will visit king to accept role on Monday and will start making cabinet appointments that afternoon
body: Andy Burnham will say Britain must be honest about the challenges it faces when he becomes its sixth prime minister in a decade on Monday, as he prepares to introduce measures to ease the cost of living and allow more North Sea fossil fuel drilling. The Labour leader is expected to start making appointments in the afternoon after going to Buckingham Palace to accept the role of prime minister from the king. Many cabinet ministers and even some of Burnham’s allies were still in the dark on Sunday about whether they would be offered positions in his top team, although there is a widespread belief Shabana Mahmood will be made chancellor and Ed Miliband foreign secretary. The chief whip, Jonathan Reynolds, is also regarded as nailed on as business secretary of a department incorporating the current science and technology department. The former cabinet minister Lou Haigh is expected to be the leading Cabinet Office minister. The former deputy prime minister Angela Rayner is said to be lined up for the job of health secretary, but sources said she would take some persuading that it was the right fit for her when her main interests are in devolution, regional inequality, local government and the cost of living. Before a policy blitz next week, Burnham’s first Downing Street address will seek to strike an optimistic but also realistic note about the problems facing Britain that must be confronted. He is expected to say the country faces a moment for “reflection and resolution”, having previously said he was willing to spend political capital on fixing major problems such as social care that will require extra funding. On measures to help people’s finances, he is likely to take steps to bring down energy bills and may target bus fares. Sources said he is also preparing to open the door to more oil and gas drilling in the North Sea in existing areas, despite a backlash from environmental campaigners and some MPs. Burnham will also say he is “acutely conscious” of being the sixth person to walk up Downing Street in 10 years, and argue that political stability is needed – in an implicit rejection of calls for an election when Labour potentially has another three years in power. He will say Britain must be honest about the challenges it faces while demonstrating that it can again be recognised around the world for effective government. The Labour leader will go on to set out his domestic priorities, saying he will deliver tangible improvements in people’s lives and give people “more breathing room” when it comes to the cost of living. Burnham has vowed to relay a 10-year plan for the country with more focus on “early investment” to ensure that the British state is “setting people up for success” rather than paying for failure. In an interview published on Sunday night, he told The Times: “You have to rethink how we run things to get a different approach to public spending and running the economy which is much more focused on early investment, early intervention, setting people up for success and much less paying for failure. “A concept I will talk about tomorrow is a 10-year plan for the country. I think that’s what it needs. I’m not saying that means I’m setting out to say I’m going to be here for 10 years, it’s more that we’re not in the right place structurally. “You go round large parts of Britain and they’re in decline still. The path we’ve got to lay is quite a long one. It can’t be done overnight. You’ve got to show people where we’re going and the steps towards it.” Burnham has not so far made any major policy commitments beyond saying he would stick broadly to Labour’s manifesto commitment of not raising VAT, national insurance or income tax, and scrapping the digital ID card programme. His allies have signalled he would be prepared to speed up the pace of current drilling in the North Sea, including potentially adding to existing fields, without approving major new fields in line with the manifesto. Donald Trump appeared to believe Burnham was preparing a significant policy shift, congratulating him on Saturday night for agreeing to go “all the way” in opening up new fields. He wrote on Truth Social: “The People of Aberdeen, in Scotland, are dancing in the streets because the new Prime Minister, Andy Burnham, has stated that he will be opening up, all the way, the invaluable North Sea Oil!” Labour’s deputy leader, Lucy Powell, had earlier said Burnham would take a more pragmatic approach and that it was a “change of emphasis” rather than a change of policy. Burnham’s greater openness on North Sea oil and gas was one factor in Miliband falling from favour as frontrunner to be chancellor, given his opposition to more drilling as energy secretary. He is being tipped instead for foreign secretary and could take a more pro-Gaza position and greater steps towards setting the UK on a pathway towards restoring foreign aid to 0.7% of national income from its current level of 0.2%. The idea was immediately seized on by Reform UK. Its leader, Nigel Farage, said: “Andy Burnham plans to spend an extra £10bn of your money on foreign aid. I will always put British people first.” Burnham’s team also declined to be drawn on his plans for Thames Water after the Sunday Times reported he was expected to push the London and south-east England utility company into a special form of administration in an intervention that could mean the state has to pay some of its running costs until a buyer is found. Powell spoke on Sunday about Burnham’s plans for increased public ownership, saying the economy had been “overly privatised” and the “utilities and essentials of life don’t work in the interest of ordinary people.” She said bills were “going up and up because they are run in the interests of private interest, not for the consumers or for taxpayers”. The Conservatives have accused Burnham of planning to put up taxes in order to fund areas that will need more money, from social care to defence. When asked about a wealth tax, the new Labour leader said he did not want to create divisions, but that in the future an option “might be having to ask for a little more”. Kemi Badenoch criticised Burnham on Sunday for not setting out his plans before Monday and not having done in-depth media interviews before taking office, describing his comments so far as “airy-fairy stuff”. The Conservative leader told the BBC she thought Burnham was “a people pleaser” rather than someone who would make tough decisions. As he prepared to hand over power, Keir Starmer released a video saying he was “so proud” to have left the country “in a much better state than we found it two years ago”.

title: Burnham’s vow to end Labour briefing wars will test his grip on the party
summary: Tensions between his aims are already there including disputes over key appointments such as chancellor
body: Andy Burnham’s vision for cleaning up Westminster politics is a utopian one: ending the dominance of the whipping system, Whitehall power hoarding and the “insidious briefing culture” of the bubble. In his first speech on becoming Labour leader, he warned his party it “won’t beat Britain’s new right if we are consumed by infighting and pulling in different directions”. Burnham is not wrong to try to weed out some of the endemic problems of factionalism in British politics that result in power struggles, rather than a focus on where agreement can be found. But the tensions between his aims are already there: to end the culture of briefing between politicians and factions jostling for ascendancy would need some extremely tough command-and-control discipline and control of the narrative from the centre. The new leader’s fledgling operation has also so far proved no different to any other when it comes to ending factionalism. Indeed, Burnham’s supporters have not got their candidate to the very top without a very large amount of briefing and organising to undermine Keir Starmer – despite his claim to have been loyal to the outgoing prime minister. Since then, there have been disputes between the right and soft left of the party, with key players falling out over who has the most input into policy, who should get which job and how policies, from North Sea drilling to immigration controls, should proceed. Ed Miliband, who helped propel Burnham to the leadership, has been the target of vicious briefing by those who feared he would be too antagonistic to business and could spook the markets if chosen as chancellor. Likewise there has been a counter-briefing operation against Shabana Mahmood, the alternative choice for chancellor, from some on the soft left who are eager to paint her as too far on the Blue Labour right to win over the party. And rival ministers have not been shy to pick up the phone to journalists to tell them why they would be better candidates for the job instead. Lucy Powell, the deputy party leader and possible deputy prime minister, told the BBC’s Sunday with Laura Kuenssberg that some of the speculation around whether Miliband would be chancellor had been “really quite horrible, to be honest, and unedifying”, blaming political journalists for whipping up a storm by reporting on the tensions. She claimed Labour had handled the leadership crisis with “consensus” around Burnham, adding: “I think we’ve given a really good account of ourselves over these last few weeks. “And maybe because political journalists haven’t had all that to write about, they’re looking for other personalities and other disagreements to write about.” However, the implication that reporting on political disagreements within the party should be suppressed runs counter to Burnham’s other desire to make Labour a broad church that can accept differing views without the need to start withdrawing the whip or expelling party members. When a leadership transfer has been made from Starmer to Burnham without the big debate of a proper contest or an election, it is not realistic to think all discussions about strategy and big jobs can be kept behind closed doors without any of the arguments being publicly surfaced. Whether on welfare changes, immigration controls or public spending, the right and left of Labour have very different views. There may be room to find common ground, but not without an airing of the disagreements and struggles over who gets to control those policy areas first. Burnham may be all powerful within Labour at the moment, and idealistic about ending factionalism and briefing wars. But he will need to maintain a very tight grip on his party and government if he thinks he will be able to decide on the future direction of the country without some big battles first.

title: Israel threatens ‘full force’ response if Iran fires missiles towards it
summary: Return to all-out regional war grows increasingly likely as US and Iran trade strikes and Trump warns of escalation
body: Israel has warned it could retaliate against Iran with “full force” if Tehran’s missiles strike within its borders, threatening a return to an all-out regional war as the ceasefire brokered by Donald Trump collapses with a new round of missile strikes across the Middle East. On Sunday, Iran launched salvoes of ballistic missiles at US-allied Arab states, targeting a power and water desalination plant for the second time in two days in Kuwait, as well as sites in Jordan and Bahrain. Air-raid sirens sounded in Amman as Jordan said it shot down three Iranian missiles targeting the country. Israel said the strikes could spill across its borders, reigniting a direct regional conflict that had been restrained even as tensions between the Trump administration and Iran soared again in recent weeks. “If Iran fires missiles at Israel, we will attack them with full force,” said the Israeli defence minister, Israel Katz, on Sunday. The country was prepared for “both defensive and offensive” action, he added. The US is expected to send dozens of additional refuelling planes to Israel as part of a reinforcement of its force posture in the region, a US official told Reuters. The US and Iran expanded their strikes, with civilian infrastructure hit, as Trump told a US media outlet that he “couldn’t care less” about Iran’s repudiation of his ceasefire agreement to end the war and reopen the strait of Hormuz. But since April, direct attacks between Iran and Israel had grown less frequent, in large part because of Trump’s pressure to maintain a ceasefire needed to reopen the strait and resume the flow of oil through the key waterway. Now, however, Trump has moved to apply maximum pressure on Iran. “The gates of HELL have now been UNLEASHED like they never could have imagined or seen before,” the US president said in a social media post on Sunday. The escalation followed the deaths of two US soldiers in Iranian strikes on a Jordanian airbase on Friday that marked one of the deadliest days for US forces since the beginning of the war. US Central Command (Centcom) has said a third soldier is missing after Iran launched drone and missile strikes following days of US strikes on Iran meant to pressure Tehran into capitulating in talks over Iran’s nuclear programme. Trump also urged Republican allies in Congress to expand a sanctions bill targeting Russia to include Iran, saying it is what the late senator Lindsey Graham would have wanted. The bill would punish purchasers of Russian oil with high tariffs, probably targeting big importers such as India. Centcom said the US had concluded an eighth day of strikes on Sunday, targeting Iran’s “military coastal surveillance and air defence facilities, maritime capabilities, and missile and drone storage sites to continue degrading Iranian military capabilities”. It also sought to “swiftly punish” the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) for the deaths of the US military personnel. Since early in the war, Trump has threatened to target Iranian civilian infrastructure, including power plants and bridges, in what many observers have said would constitute a war crime. The US has denied targeting civilian infrastructure, but photos and videos have shown considerable destruction at civilian sites in the last few days. Tehran on Sunday said more than 40 people had died in attacks this month. Iran also accused the US of striking a nuclear power plant under construction in the south-western city of Darkhovin. The International Atomic Energy Agency said the site did not house any nuclear material but its director general, Rafael Grossi, urged “military restraint in the vicinity of all nuclear-related sites”. Iran has begun targeting crucial desalination plants in countries such as Kuwait, which are extremely arid and rely on the plants to produce potable water for the population. Kuwait said the Iranian strike had sparked a fire and damaged electricity generators at the plant, potentially disrupting power supplies. The Kuwaiti ⁠foreign ministry said the repeated targeting of civilian facilities amounted to a dangerous escalation and violated ‌international law, the UN charter and UN security council resolution 2817. It said Iran bore full legal and moral responsibility ‌for the attack on the plant and its consequences. Jordan summoned ⁠Iran’s ⁠chargé d’affaires in Amman ⁠over what ⁠it called “unjustified ‌and ‌blatant Iranian attacks” ‌and “provocative and inflammatory statements targeting the ‌kingdom’s territory”. Tehran has also increased pressure in the strait of Hormuz. Iran’s government-aligned Tasnim news agency said on Sunday that two ships had had an “accident” after attempting to traverse the waterway via an “unsafe route” not officially sanctioned by Tehran. “Vessels that are influenced by the words of the Americans and enter unsafe routes will certainly face accidents,” the IRGC said, adding that two other ships had turned back after the incident. In a statement on Saturday, Iran’s supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, warned that the US would face “unforgettable lessons” if it continued its attacks.

title: Fossil fuels, utilities and housing: what we know about Andy Burnham’s plans
summary: Incoming prime minister has set out distinctly leftwing vision while also promising to be pro-business
body: As an incoming prime minister promising huge change, one of the strongest criticisms Andy Burnham has faced so far is that he has failed to provide the details of what he plans to do. The new Labour leader, who will be made prime minister on Monday, has thus far set out a distinctly leftwing vision, while also pledging to be “pro-business”. But in the absence of leadership contest hustings or opportunities for the media to subject him to probing interviews, there are gaps in what is known about his plans for government. Here is what we do know so far. Public control of utilities Burnham has pledged to take more public control of key utilities such as water, a move that appears likely to begin with Britain’s largest water company, Thames Water. He is also understood to be considering radical plans that could cut household energy bills by £130 a year and make running a heat pump cheaper than a gas boiler. The proposals would change the way household gas is charged and remove some policy levies from bills, at a cost of £3.2bn a year to the taxpayer. Thames Water, which serves 16 million customers and has been on the brink of collapse for several years, will meanwhile be put into a special form of administration until a buyer is found, according to the Sunday Times. Expect action, too, on transport policy. Burnham has said he would be informed by the transformation of the Greater Manchester bus network during his time as mayor, which included taking the system back under public control from private operators. Drill, baby, drill Speculation is rife that Burnham will announce fresh plans for drilling in the North Sea. The Labour leader’s team has not publicly confirmed the details of its North Sea policies and whether they would involve new exploration licences or “tiebacks”, where offshore oil and gas fields are connected to already approved production facilities via pipelines. In its 2024 manifesto, Labour pledged to honour existing exploration licences but not issue new ones. The Guardian understands that the tieback arrangements would allow new drilling to take place without technically breaching the manifesto commitment, although any new activity is likely to anger some backbenchers and climate campaigners. Sticking with Mahmood’s immigration changes Even before his Makerfield byelection victory made him an MP again, Burnham’s allies had briefed that he would be supporting Shabana Mahmood’s controversial changes to the immigration system. On Monday, and back in the House of Commons, he supported the government’s asylum changes, voting for legislation that has divided Labour MPs over plans to tighten the immigration system and reshape the appeals process. While he has avoided laying out specific policies in the area in key speeches, he has said recently that net migration needs to fall further and spoken of how voters in Makerfield “raised their concerns about immigration”. Digital ID Burnham is expected to scrap Keir Starmer’s plans for digital ID cards in a “reset of priorities”, redirecting resources earmarked for the scheme towards tackling the cost of living. The proposals were outlined at the weekend in an attempt to distance the incoming prime minister from unpopular choices made by his predecessor. A petition opposing the introduction of digital ID cards when it was announced last year attracted about 3m signatures. Keir Starmer first announced the digital ID scheme last September, as part of a strategy to combat illegal working. It was to be introduced by 2029. Scrapping the Department for Science, but appointing a minister for AI Burnham’s team have asked officials to draw up plans to abolish the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) as part of a wider Whitehall shake-up. However, while the plans have triggered an angry backlash from MPs, Whitehall officials and others, a new cabinet on Monday is expected to include a minister for artificial intelligence for the first time. The proposals in relation to the DSIT would hand over much of science and technology policy to a more powerful business department, to be led by the chief whip, Jonathan Reynolds. Grasping the social care ‘nettle’ The prime-minister-in-waiting highlighted fixing social care as a priority, vowing last week to “grasp the nettle” of adult social care reform. Burnham first tried to change the social care system when he was Labour’s health secretary in 2009. At the time, he proposed a levy on estates to pay for universal social care, but he has since talked about replacing inheritance tax with a progressive “care levy” to fund a national care service. Speaking to the Guardian on the campaign trail last month, he said: “I personally would look at all of the kind of implications of that in relation to inheritance tax and care charges and everything. I wouldn’t flinch from it.” Council house building Burnham has said he wants the government led by him to “oversee the biggest council house building programme since the postwar period”. The challenge is a major one, the scale of which is seemingly not lost on Burnham, who linked the loss of almost 1.5m council homes since the 1980s to the number of families on social housing waiting lists nationwide rising to a similar level. In a hint of other plans, he has also said that high costs are having a “ruinous impact” on the government finances amid record levels of taxpayer support allocated to housing, much of which flows to private landlords.

title: Thames Water creditors seek talks with Burnham as nationalisation looms
summary: Consortium preparing for potential legal battle amid reports new PM could put the company into temporary public ownership
body: The group of investors pursuing a rescue bid for Thames Water have said they are willing to discuss greater public control but are also preparing for a potential multi-billion pound legal battle amid reports that Andy Burnham could temporarily nationalise the company. London & Valley Water (L&VW), a consortium of 100 institutional investors that hold £17bn of the company’s £21bn debt, has said it is open to government involvement with Thames Water, but indicated that this does not include public ownership in the struggling company. Mike McTighe, the corporate troubleshooter who is leading the governance overhaul and building the new board proposed for Thames, Britain’s biggest water company, said the consortium wanted to work constructively with Burnham when he takes over as prime minister. “We are keen to meet new ministers as soon as possible to discuss how we can work together in the best interests of customers, including by enhancing public control of the company’s operations,” he said. “We will work with Andy Burnham, his government and local authority leaders to rebuild confidence in Thames Water and the wider sector.” The comments by McTighe, who is likely to take over as Thames Water’s chair if the consortium is able to get its £10bn rescue deal approved by the government, follow reports that Burnham is planning to transfer the company into a special administration regime (SAR), a form of temporary public ownership, after he takes over as prime minister. Burnham said last month that there should be “greater public control” of Thames Water and told the Guardian this could mean nationalisation. The SAR proposal would transfer the costs of running the company to the taxpayer, with Thames Water’s creditors claiming the bill could come to £2bn. “If it is going to cost the taxpayer £2bn to keep the company afloat then the taxpayer needs to receive something in return; that means control, so that we can fix the company and secure the water supply for thousands of families and businesses,” a Burnham ally told The Sunday Times. L&VW, which includes fund managers such as Apollo Global Management, Elliott Management, Farallon Capital Management and Silver Point Capital, has also moved to strengthen its legal position in order to be prepared if Thames is nationalised. The consortium has hired top litigation and disputes firm Pallas Partners to work alongside Akin Gump, the law firm advising the consortium on the terms of their restructuring proposals. “Creditors are assessing all potential routes that the situation regarding Thames Water may play out,” said one person close to the consortium. “They want, and need, to be ready. There is no legal action being taken at this point. This is purely a precautionary measure.” Earlier this month, Thames Water’s creditors were said to be still willing to pursue their bid for the debt-laden company, even if Burnham were to bring it into temporary nationalisation. “The consortium is trying to pursue a solvent restructuring,” said the person. “This would avoid a taxpayer funded administration process and help creditors recover as much as possible. It can bid alongside any others, but that prolongs everything.” The future of Thames, which serves 16 million customers in London and the Thames valley and is buckling under interest payments on debt it has run up since privatisation, will be one of the most pressing issues in Burnham’s in-tray when he enters Downing Street. “We remain ready and willing to recapitalise Thames Water, return it to investment grade, and begin the long process of turning it around,” said McTighe. “We urgently need government engagement to begin that process.” McTighe is the chairman of Openreach, BT Group’s infrastructure arm, and previously chaired the Daily Telegraph’s publisher. The lenders have been trying to take ownership and bring it out of administration after a failed attempt to sell the utility to the US investment group KKR last year. However, the creditors’ plans were thrown into doubt last month when Emma Reynolds, the environment secretary, wrote to the regulator, Ofwat, voicing concerns about the terms of the deal.

title: US boxer Hannah Rapp struck and killed by driver while cycling, police say
summary: Investigators arrest 31-year-old man identified as Charles Medina in connection with death of Rapp who was 26
body: US boxer Hannah Rapp, who in June fought for the World Boxing Council (WBC) women’s featherweight championship, was killed on Saturday while she bicycled in Texas after a motorist struck her with his car, according to authorities. Rapp was 26, and investigators arrested a 31-year-old man identified as Charles Medina in connection with her death, the sheriff’s office of Brazos county, Texas, said in a statement. The agency said Rapp and a fellow bicyclist were riding on a local road when Medina passed them in his car, stopped, and drove his vehicle in reverse. Medina then hit Rapp, and she was pronounced dead after emergency responders brought her to a hospital. Brazos deputies said they booked Medina on a count of manslaughter, which Texas law defines as recklessly causing a person’s death. The sheriff’s office did not provide additional details, saying the “isolated” case remained under active and ongoing investigation. Word of Rapp’s death prompted tributes from her colleagues in professional boxing. “The loss of Hannah Rapp leaves us heartbroken,” WBC president Mauricio Sulaimán said in a prepared statement. “She was an exceptional boxer, but above all, an invaluable member of our boxing family. “We join in the grief that overwhelms her loved ones, her team, and all those who had the privilege of knowing and supporting her throughout her brilliant career.” Rapp had registered eight victories – including five by knockout – and drawn once during her first nine pro career bouts. For her 10th outing, the Yorktown, Indiana, native challenged Tiara Brown of Fort Myers, Florida, for her world title belt in the WBC’s 126lb (57.2kg) weight class. Brown retained her belt with a unanimous decision win at Florida’s Caribe Royal Orlando resort. Nonetheless, on Saturday, the champion wrote on social media that Rapp “was the best dance partner I have ever faced as a pro”. “We fought a GREAT fight for the fans from the [first] bell to the last,” Brown wrote. “It was my honor to share the ring with her.” Medina’s bond amount was not immediately set, and he remained in custody on Sunday morning, Brazos jail records show. Those jail records also showed prior arrests for Medina on counts such as deadly conduct by discharging a firearm, assault causing bodily injury, and reckless driving. Dispositions for those cases weren’t immediately available. Medina could face between two and 20 years in prison under Texas law if eventually convicted of manslaughter in Rapp’s death.

title: Andy Burnham must raise the spirits of a disillusioned public
summary: Letters: Readers give their views on Britain’s new prime minister
body: How fortuitous for Andy Burnham that just as Keir Starmer departs from Downing Street, the threat from the menacing presence of Nigel Farage is also beginning to dissolve (Editorial, 15 July). Burnham is an unknown quantity to most of the public so he needs to define who he is, what he believes in and what he will do about the things people care about most – the cost of living, the housing market and the parlous state of public services. In stark contrast to Keir Starmer’s cautious and forensic persona, Burnham is a skilled operator, familiar with the rough cut and thrust of politics, and a track record of fusing the energy and talents of people from diverse perspectives to work towards a common good. Every one of these attributes, together with a great deal of good fortune and Burnham’s renowned skills in communicating, will be needed to raise the spirits of an embattled, disillusioned and sceptical public. Starmer’s departure from office will be to no avail if it is not followed swiftly by the public feeling an increasing sense of hope and wellbeing. Peter Riddle Wirksworth, Derbyshire • As a former Labour member who resigned this year following the announcement of reforms to refugee status, I felt Andy Burnham’s Labour leadership speech was inspiring and full of hope (Burnham declares he is ready to ‘beat Britain’s new right’ as he becomes Labour leader, 17 July). I noted that the speech didn’t provide any deep insight into his foreign policy thinking. Keir Starmer’s two-year reign has improved EU relationships, played a key role in shaping Europe’s policy towards Ukraine and improved trade agreements with several nations. Mr Burnham and the new foreign secretary need to maintain the pace and focus on these three critical issues. Improved trade with the EU provides new opportunities for young people and businesses. Ukrainian citizens, the EU and the UK need to know the war is not placed on a lower level of priority by the UK government given Burnham’s overflowing in-tray. Paresh Motla Thame, Oxfordshire • Peter Walker (Analysis, 17 July) identifies “an almost Johnsonian sense of mischief” in Andy Burnham’s acceptance speech, but which Johnson is he referencing? Until relatively recently, I would have assumed that he meant the 18th-century lexicographer Samuel Johnson. If it’s a latter-day Boris Johnson, perhaps the dictionary definition of “Johnsonian” needs updating. Pity Dr Johnson isn’t around to do the job. Steve Newman Sunderland • Before Andy Burnham starts nationalising stuff “to undo the Thatcherism of the 1980s” and we party like it’s 1966, perhaps he could have a word with the likes of Greg Jackson at Octopus Energy and Jeff Bezos at Amazon instead of assuming that the state running utilities is the holy grail. I for one I don’t want my energy to come from Ed Miliband. Charles Foster Chalfont St Peter, Buckinghamshire • If Andy Burnham is serious about recovering the Labour values of 40 years ago, I look forward to the return of clause IV to the party constitution. Steve King London • Should Andy Burnham appoint a minister for drought? It worked in 1976. Hilary Bird Kilndown, Kent • So Andy Burnham has “a plan”. Or is that a cunning plan? Dr Phil Dawson Newcastle upon Tyne • Have an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Please email us your letter and it will be considered for publication in our letters section.

title: Israel’s brutality cannot continue in plain sight
summary: Letters: Gwyn Daniel and Phil Gaunt respond to an article by Nesrine Malik about the horrors taking place in Israel’s prisons
body: Nesrine Malik’s incisive piece (In Israel’s prisons, torture and death have become a norm that it barely tries to hide, 13 July) lays bare the fact that all the illegal detentions, deaths, torture and sexual abuse in Israeli prisons are not aberrations from a legal system, but deeply embedded in the practices and intentions of the state. She mentions the psychological dimension to deliberately eroding the capacity of Palestinian civil society to exert any kind of autonomy. As mental health practitioners, working alongside our Palestinian co-professionals, we have long recorded the ways in which practices of degradation, humiliation and debilitation constitute attacks on minds and relationships as well as bodies. They are designed to undermine the fabric of family, social and community life, and to destroy those systems of connectivity upon which all citizens, especially children, are dependent if they are to flourish. The carceral system is part of a set of state interventions which not only detain, murder, maim and mutilate Palestinian bodies, but also conduct an unrelenting assault on their growth, dignity, personhood and identity. Malik is right to highlight the extent to which western governments and most mainstream media act as if it is the “extremist fringes” rather than the state itself that should be criticised or sanctioned. In doing so, they fail to heed the pronouncements from Israeli government representatives themselves and do a profound disservice to the victims of this violent, racist and unrestrained regime. Gwyn Daniel UK and International Palestine Mental Health Networks • Thank you once again for Nesrine Malik’s continued efforts to draw attention to the brutality that has been inflicted on the Palestinian people. While Andy Burnham has acknowledged that the British government’s response to the Israel bombardment and mass killing in Gaza “didn’t get it right”, there have since been innumerable atrocities in Gaza and the West Bank, even after a ceasefire was declared. Sanctions on illegal settlers who attack and kill Palestinians will not be enough – the Israeli government provides them with immunity, leaves the people of Gaza camped amid devastation and detains thousands of Palestinians without trial in reportedly dreadful conditions. The UK and Europe need to act far more decisively regarding trade with Israel and take meaningful steps to ending the illegal occupation of Palestine. Phil Gaunt Dumbarton • Have an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Please email us your letter and it will be considered for publication in our letters section.

title: ‘He’s a war criminal’: Mamdani exploring whether he can arrest Netanyahu if he visits New York
summary: NYC mayor says in Times interview that’s he’s in ‘active conversation’ with city’s law department about the matter
body: New York City mayor Zohran Mamdani has said he was reviewing whether his administration could arrest Benjamin Netanyahu if as expected the Israeli prime minister visits the city for the UN general assembly in September. Mamdani’s comments on Saturday’s episode of the New York Times’s the Interview podcast echoed ones he had made to the publication as he successfully ran for mayor last fall. “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” Mamdani said, referring to the Dutch city that is home to the international criminal court, which issued an arrest warrant for Netanyahu in November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza. “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.” Mamdani said it was unclear to him whether he has the legal authority to order the New York police department (NYPD), which he oversees, to detain a foreign leader like Netanyahu. But he said he was in “an active conversation” with the city’s law department about the matter. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” Mamdani added. “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.” The warrant which the ICC issued for the arrest of Netanyahu and other Israeli leaders accused them of war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”. During his mayoral campaign, Mamdani characterized Israel’s military actions in Gaza as a genocide and Netanyahu as a war criminal, telling the New York Times in September that he would order NYPD to arrest Netanyahu in the event he ever set foot there. He also condemned the 7 October 2023 Hamas attacks on Israel as a “horrific war crime”. The Times report back then cited legal experts who judged that having Netanyahu arrested “would be a practical impossibility” for Mamdani – and could bring him into direct conflict with the US’s federal government. The US is not a party to the ICC and does not recognize its authority. And in February 2025, early in his presidency, Donald Trump ordered economic sanctions against the court, maintaining that it lacked jurisdiction over the US or Israel. Trumps’s US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, then recently vowed to “dismantle” the court, accusing it of “waging a war against our country, not with bullets or missiles” but with “the force of so-called international law”. Netanyahu, who faces a tough re-election campaign in October, brushed off Mamdani’s arrest threats in a recent radio appearance, saying he was not concerned. Though Mamdani denies the accusation, Netanyahu also alleged Mamdani supports Hamas. “He’s with the terror actors,” Netanyahu told New York’s WABC. “And I think the problem is that he doesn’t recognize and he doesn’t care that those who hate the Jews and Israel ultimately hate America.” Israel’s ambassador to the UN, Danny Danon, accused Mamdani – New York’s first Muslim mayor – of failing to confront rising antisemitism in his city. Mamdani has countered such accusations by noting that he has increased funding for an office to combat hate crimes. “Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu will come to New York, address the United Nations General Assembly with pride, and stand before the world to state Israel’s truth and its unwavering right to defend its citizens,” Danon said. Trump’s ambassador to the UN, Mike Waltz, also dismissed Mamdani’s threat as “pure political theater”, saying the US is not a member of the ICC. World leaders from United Nations member-states are expected to travel to New York in September for the body’s annual session. Netanyahu has often participated in this event in previous years. “We’re not talking about a personal assessment of Benjamin Netanyahu,” Mamdani told the Times’ podcast. “We’re talking about the international criminal court and the fact that they have a warrant out for this prime minister’s arrest, and I think it’s important to talk about the weight that that has as a charge and that also, as the mayor of New York City, I’ll be following the laws of New York City.” Mamdani’s comments on Saturday came as his stance on Israel is becoming less fringe within the Democratic party. Close to half of US House Democrats on Wednesday voted to strip $3.3bn in military aid to Israel. It was not enough to pass the measure. But it signified a buckling of unconditional support for Israel, a shift that is also reflected in public opinion polling. Asked by the Times about the political weight he places on Israel and support for it by the US, Mamdani said ongoing war in Gaza was motivating voters throughout the country, including when his endorsed candidates swept House races in New York in June. “It is hard to find a more bankrupt policy approach than what our country has done to Gaza and to Palestine,” he said.

title: A rhyme to recall rising temperatures
summary: Brief letters: The new normal | Cool school | Italian idiom | Word Wheel | Covid failings
body: Your article (‘Unprecedented’ changes in UK climate are normalizing extremes, report says, 15 July) reminded me of the rhyme to help people convert from fahrenheit to centigrade when I was a schoolgirl in the late 60s. This was “five, 10 and 21 – winter, spring and summer sun”, denoting the then average temperatures. Maybe this could be updated for the 2020s. I was thinking of “10, 15, 28 – reduce emissions before too late”. Liz Haggart Bearsted, Kent • Of all the inequalities in education, access to tree shade would not be high on my list (Private schools offer 41% more tree shade than state schools in England, 16 July). It’s a poor metric, as if a state school is forced to sell an open playing field, the proportion of its site covered by trees could increase. I would look at the extent of air-conditioned classrooms first. Peter West London • Rhiannon Lucy Cosslett writes that learning languages slows brain ageing (12 July). I enjoy learning Italian idioms. The one to match “it’s another kettle of fish” is e un altro paio di maniche (“it’s another pair of sleeves”). Still trying at 88. Les Bonney Cheltenham, Gloucestershire • As a Crystal Palace fan, I was perturbed that among the words I found in the Word Wheel (13 July print edition) were “eagle” and “relegated”. Is this a worrying portent? John Bryant Topsham, Exeter • The letter from Laura Matthew (As a frontline worker, my anger over the Tories’ handling of Covid has not abated, 16 July) should be engraved in stone and made a national monument. Leila Roberts Mickley, North Yorkshire • Have an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Please email us your letter and it will be considered for publication in our letters section.

title: Australia’s life dissatisfaction levels double as ‘wellbeing poverty’ reaches new highs
summary: ABS data shows one in 10 people are not satisfied with life – but community cohesion and trust lift happiness scores
body: Nearly 2.2 million Australians 15 years and older are living below the wellbeing poverty line, with the share of people reporting very low life satisfaction doubling over the past decade. In 2025, 9.7%, or about one in 10 Australians, rated their general satisfaction with life at four or below out of 10, according to Guardian Australia’s analysis of the Australian Bureau of Statistics’s (ABS) general social survey. That compares with one in 20, or 4.8%, reporting very low overall life satisfaction in 2014. The initial surge of Australians reporting dissatisfaction with their lives came during 2020’s Covid lockdowns and has persisted over the intervening years amid soaring cost of living. The ABS data echoes other surveys, such as Westpac’s consumer confidence index, which has languished at around 50-year lows since failing to recover from the pandemic blow. Amid an explosion of support for far-right and populist politics, those reporting life satisfaction scores of four or below are more likely to feel alienated and disempowered in their community, and express lower support for multiculturalism, the ABS figures show. Only 15% of people experiencing wellbeing poverty feel they have a say on important matters affecting their communities, against the average of 32% across all Australians. The proportion of people who feel they have a voice in their community increases to a high of 46% among those reporting overall life satisfaction of nine or above. As One Nation’s leader, Pauline Hanson, makes headlines with her advocacy for a “monocultural” society, the ABS data show just over 60% of people who score in the lowest group on life satisfaction agree that it is a good thing for society to be composed of different cultures. That share is far below the average of 80% among Australians, and the approaching 90% among those reporting the highest life satisfaction. Stagnation and stagflation National living standards, as measured by household disposable income per household adjusted for inflation, are at the same level as in mid-2020. Nor is the outlook encouraging: analysts expect the national economy to drop back into a per capita recession amid a stagflationary backdrop of low growth and high inflation. Shane Oliver, AMP’s chief economist, said lower-income households had been hit particularly hard by the huge rise in prices for essentials, such as electricity, insurance and healthcare. “Consumer confidence seems to be chronically stuck around recessionary levels,” Oliver said. Guardian Australia has previously reported research showing that wages for the highest paid workers over the past decade had grown at more than twice the pace of those earning the least. Oliver also pointed to extensive literature on the economics of happiness that satisfaction with life does not track higher with material wealth past a certain middle-income threshold. Sign up for the Breaking News Australia email Oliver said it was likely that the recent plunge in life satisfaction related to thwarted hopes and unmet expectations – much of which he believes can be traced back to dwindling hopes of home ownership. “I suspect housing is the single biggest problem. If we managed that issue better, that would go a long way to addressing this issue,” he said. Anthea Hancocks is the chief executive of the Scanlon Foundation Research Institute, an independent body that focuses on social cohesion. “We know that people’s economic circumstances have a really big impact on their sense of social cohesion because it impacts their sense of self-worth,” Hancocks said. “If you feel like nobody cares and you don’t really matter, then that would make you feel like you can’t trust other people or trust institutions. And that tends to lead to not participating in their community or talking to people, so it has a big effect,” she said. Social media has had “a huge impact” on Australians’ lives and how they perceive the world and their place in it, she said. Despite these undercurrents of dissatisfaction and growing financial pressures, Hancocks says social cohesion nationally has proved resilient over recent years. Scanlon’s social cohesion index has stayed steady at 78 over the past three years, although it remains considerably below the recent peak of 92 in 2021. Hancocks said connecting people to their neighbours and community was key to lifting life satisfaction. “We know that regardless of your economic circumstances, whether you are struggling or you say you are prosperous, if you live in a highly cohesive neighbourhood, then your level of happiness doubles,” she said. “That is where our strength comes from now, and most communities are actively doing things in this space. “That won’t solve the problem but building that trust is an essential component. Building national pride simply in being Australian – all of those things will help and can be the fundamental element to help people feel like someone has got their back.”

title: Why a modest US interest rate rise won’t change much for most businesses
summary: Rates may rise, but don’t be too concerned – a small increase is unlikely to change major decisions on investment or hiring
body: With the appointment of a new Federal Reserve chair, the latest concern is that interest rates will go up in the next few months. That decision will depend on many factors, including inflation, jobs and the economy’s overall growth. But when that happens, watch out! The media will go nuts and the president will go ballistic. Everyone treats every Fed meeting as an economic earthquake. But for most small businesses, it isn’t. A 25-basis-point increase doesn’t meaningfully change borrowing costs for most established businesses. In fact, it changes nothing at all. But say the Fed increases the federal funds rate by that amount. The result would likely be an increase in the prime rate to 7%. Most of my clients – small businesses – don’t pay the “prime” rate because, well … they’re small businesses and are generally more risky investments. So they often pay 1% or 2% over prime. Will this interest rate increase change their investment, borrowing and hiring decisions? No, it won’t. A business borrowing $500,000 from a bank for a five-year equipment loan, for example, would see their annual payments go up from $120,942 to $121,658. Even if the bank’s prime rate jumped to 8.5%, their annual payment would only rise to $123,099. This is not enough of a difference to discourage my clients from moving forward with a financing project. The reality is that capital markets – even for small businesses – are pretty stable right now. Some may say that startups could be squeezed. But, like always, that depends on what kind of startup we’re talking about. If you’re in tech – particularly AI – money is falling from the sky. Venture capital funding, particularly to tech companies and anyone that mentions AI, skyrocketed 51% last year to a whopping $320bn. If – like most small businesses – you’re not a VC candidate, then you’ll find that banks are still interested in you too. The Small Business Administration has increased credit availability for many small businesses – particularly manufacturers – and is guaranteeing a significantly higher level of bank loans than in years past. Small business loan approvals held firm at around 52% last year (up from 46% in 2021), according to the Federal Reserve. Financing firm Biz2Credit reported recently that debt repayment volume increased 24% and debt coverage improved from 0.57x in Q1 2025 to 1.40x in Q1 2026, “suggesting that stronger [small and medium-sized businesses] have demonstrated a greater capacity to manage their monthly obligations”. What about consumers? Their spending is the main factor behind small business growth, and if they’re maxed out on credit that would reverberate across the economy. Still, so far, so good. Consumer spending rose sharply last month to a rate significantly above inflation. Delinquency rates and bank charge-offs for delinquent balances on credit cards have both been falling each quarter since 2024. Consumer credit remained on “solid footing” in May, according to a widely watched credit scoring agency report, with the data suggesting that consumers have “largely adapted to a prolonged higher-rate environment despite rising household expenses and the return of student loan payments”. This doesn’t mean that both businesses and consumers aren’t without their financing challenges. There are 34m small businesses and more than 360 million people that live here, so there’s bound to be a not insignificant percentage of people and companies that struggle to raise funds or pay their bills. Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase, the country’s largest bank, recently shared his concerns that the next credit cycle (a recurring economic response for when borrowers struggle to repay, defaults and losses rise, lenders tighten standards sharply, and credit becomes scarce) will hit likely harder than expected, pointing to $5.1tn in leveraged finance (private credit, high-yield bonds, syndicated loans) as the key stress point. Too much of an interest rate increase could certainly trigger that. But your average plumbing company that borrows $500,000 for equipment isn’t going to be much concerned about whether to buy that machine based on a quarter-point rate increase. They decide based on whether they believe demand will justify the investment. Another cause for concern is the rise in US bankruptcies. The American Bankruptcy Institute recently reported that small business bankruptcy filings “jumped” 67% this past quarter over last year thanks to “persistent inflation, elevated interest rates and geopolitical instability” and warned that the more “immediate constraint is access to credit” as some lenders are becoming more cautious in extending financing due the uncertainties that continue to challenge the US and global economies. However, and like Dimon, both Brian Moynhian and Charles Scharf – who run Bank of America and Wells Fargo, respectively – are not yet raising red flags. In its latest earnings release, Moynihan said his bank “remains watchful of evolving risks” but so far has seen “healthy client activity, including solid consumer spending and stable asset quality, indicating a resilient American economy”. Scharf reported similar findings, saying that “while markets have been volatile, we still see continued resiliency in the underlying economy and the financial health of the consumers and businesses we serve remains strong, though the impact of higher oil prices will likely take some time to materialize”. So will the Fed increase interest rates? Maybe. But I wouldn’t be too concerned. For many established small businesses, a modest increase is unlikely to alter major investment or hiring decisions.
